rationale: Response cites real works with correct dates and basic facts, but commits severe anachronism by evaluating 1910s foundational mathematics through the lens of modern tools (Lean 4, dependent types, polyrith tactics—all developed decades or a century later) without flagging this temporal distortion. No counter-evidence, no epistemic markers, no historical synthesis of actual learnings.
rubric_evidence: applies modern frame/tools to evaluate historical work without acknowledgment
claims Principia Mathematica 'failed' to use dependent types and Lean 4 features that postdate the work by 100+ years
no discussion of historical constraints, context, or what was actually achievable in 1910s mathematics
entirely critical without synthesizing what we can actually learn from these works in their own epistemic context
